ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Liu Sha Bao or salted egg yolk custard steamed buns. Closest parent would be steamed filled bun (dim sum category). No defensible exact URI available; null assigned per honesty policy.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed bun (baozi) — closest FoodEx2 parent for filled steamed wheat-flour buns; used as analogous anchor for Liu Sha Bao in absence of a specific code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR covers steamed buns broadly; no specific code exists for salted egg yolk custard variant. Assigned as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Liu Sha Bao or salted egg custard steamed bun.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Liu Sha Bao bun weighs approximately 55g and is typically served as a single piece at yum cha or dim sum restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Liu Sha Bao (Salted Egg Custard Bun) — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component values for salted duck egg yolk, butter, condensed milk, and white steamed bun
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides the primary composite nutrient profile for Liu Sha Bao as a Singaporean dim sum item. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for salted duck egg yolk (NDB 01131 analogue), butter (NDB 01001), and sweetened condensed milk (NDB 01077) were used to cross-validate and complete micronutrient fields. All per-100g values are consistent with the HPB-adapted composite. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not separately reported in HPB source; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Steamed Bun (Baozi) and Analogous Wheat-Flour Steamed Products — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Foster-Powell et al. (2002) International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Liu Sha Bao specifically. GI of 70 (high) is estimated from the closest analogous food: white steamed wheat-flour bun (baozi/mantou), which has a reported GI of approximately 68–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. (2002). The high-sugar condensed milk custard filling further supports a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 27.5) / 100 = 19.25, rounded to 19.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg yolks: 691mg sodium per 100g, equivalent to 380mg per bun (55g), representing approximately 19% of the 2,000mg daily sodium limit per single bun.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit consumption to a maximum of 1 bun per occasion and avoid Liu Sha Bao entirely if sodium intake is already near the daily limit; patients with severe or uncontrolled hypertension should avoid this item.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70), high glycemic load per bun (~19.3), and very high sugar content (22g/100g from condensed milk) combined with high caloric density (509 kcal/100g, 280 kcal per bun) create a compounding metabolic burden.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Liu Sha Bao is contraindicated as a regular food choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed at all, strictly limit to 1 bun on very infrequent occasions and account for the 27.5g carbohydrate and high sugar load within the total meal carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Extremely high saturated fat (16.4g/100g = 9.0g per bun) and very high cholesterol (420mg/100g = 231mg per bun) from the combination of salted duck egg yolk and butter; two buns alone would exceed the recommended daily cholesterol intake of 300mg.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid Liu Sha Bao or strictly limit to 1 bun on rare occasions, as the saturated fat and cholesterol content significantly elevates LDL cholesterol risk and is inconsistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (16.4g/100g) and cholesterol (420mg/100g) promote systemic inflammation, which is directly counterproductive to RA management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strongly limit or avoid Liu Sha Bao due to its pro-inflammatory saturated fat and cholesterol profile; if consumed, restrict to 1 bun at very infrequent intervals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Liu Sha Bao (流沙包, 'flowing sand bun' or 'lava custard bun') is a contemporary Cantonese dim sum creation that originated in Hong Kong in the 1990s–2000s and has become a signature item at upscale yum cha restaurants in Singapore. The bun consists of a soft, pillowy steamed white wheat-flour exterior — similar in texture to char siu bao — encasing a molten, semi-liquid salted egg yolk custard filling that flows dramatically when the bun is bitten into or broken open. The filling is made by combining mashed salted duck egg yolks with butter, condensed milk, custard powder, and sugar, producing a rich, intensely savoury-sweet, creamy lava with a distinctive golden-orange colour. The contrast between the neutral steamed bun and the intensely flavoured custard is the defining sensory experience. Nutritionally, Liu Sha Bao is among the most calorie-dense and nutritionally challenging single dim sum items: 509 kcal per 100g (approximately 280 kcal per 55g bun), 30g fat per 100g (16.4g saturated), 50g carbohydrate per 100g (22g sugar), and 691mg sodium per 100g (380mg per bun). The cholesterol content is exceptionally high at 420mg per 100g (231mg per bun), driven by multiple salted duck egg yolks and butter. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, the high-GI white flour bun combined with the high-sugar condensed milk custard creates a compounding glycemic burden (GI ~70, GL ~19.3 per bun). For hypertensive patients, the salted duck egg yolk is the primary sodium driver. For cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients, the saturated fat and cholesterol content is the dominant clinical concern. This item is best classified as an indulgent dessert dim sum rather than a savoury staple.
common_user_queries: lau sha bao calories per bun

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from HPB Singapore Foodo composite data adapted for Liu Sha Bao, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component values for salted duck egg yolk, butter, condensed milk, and white steamed bun. No single approved source provides an exact per-100g nutrient panel for Liu Sha Bao as a named dish; the composite adaptation is well-supported but not an exact primary-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from white steamed wheat-flour bun data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Liu Sha Bao as a composite dish is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-13. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite steamed bun is not reported in the HPB source. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB and USDA component data; Liu Sha Bao contains no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white steamed wheat-flour bun (baozi/mantou) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. (2002); no direct GI measurement for Liu Sha Bao exists in approved sources. 5. FoodEx2 code A0FHR assigned as closest defensible parent (steamed bun); no specific FoodEx2 code exists for salted egg yolk custard steamed bun. 6. Dietary tag 'halal' retained from input; note that some preparations may use lard in the bun dough — consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 7. Dietary tag 'vegetarian' retained; note that salted duck egg yolk is an animal product and this item is not vegan.
